_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan Part B (paragraph matching), batch A3 REWORKED to 450-650 words. 7 paragraphs (A-G), each 4-5 sentences; 5 statements are strong paraphrases. answers = 0-based indices; paras=7. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage:
[A] In the several decades since the very first satellites were launched into orbit, the narrow band of space just above our planet, once empty and pristine, has grown steadily and alarmingly crowded with the discarded debris of our own restless activity. What began as a mere handful of objects has become a vast, fast-moving swarm of junk that now poses a genuine threat to the very space technology on which modern life has come to depend.

[B] The litter has many distinct sources. It includes satellites that have died and been abandoned in orbit, the spent upper stages of the rockets that originally carried them aloft, flecks of paint and frozen droplets of fuel, and, most troublingly of all, the countless fragments thrown violently off in every direction whenever two such objects happen to collide with one another at tremendous speed.

[C] The real danger lies not in the size of this debris but in its astonishing velocity. Travelling at many kilometres every second, far faster than any rifle bullet, even a tiny fleck of paint or a single stray bolt carries enough concentrated energy to puncture a spacecraft's hull or to disable a large, expensive and fully functioning satellite in a single devastating instant.

[D] Worse still, the problem has a frightening capacity to feed upon itself. One high-speed collision can create many thousands of new fragments, and each of those fragments is itself capable of causing yet further collisions, in a runaway chain reaction that scientists now soberly call the Kessler syndrome, after the researcher who first described its terrifying logic.

[E] Cleaning up the orbital mess is at last being seriously attempted. Engineers around the world are designing and testing a whole range of experimental methods, from nets and harpoons that would physically capture large pieces of junk, to ground-based lasers intended to nudge smaller fragments gently downward until they re-enter the atmosphere and burn up harmlessly.

[F] Yet the question of responsibility remains stubbornly unclear. No single nation owns the orbital commons, and so no single nation owns the problem; reaching agreement on exactly who ought to pay for the costly business of removing whose particular junk, much of it now decades old, has proved to be a genuinely difficult diplomatic and legal puzzle. The major space-faring powers, understandably reluctant to spend their own scarce money cleaning up debris that other countries launched decades earlier, have so far failed to agree on any binding rules, and the awkward underlying truth is that everyone alike benefits from a clean and usable orbit while almost no one actually wishes to pay the considerable bill for achieving it.

[G] Unless the situation is actively and cooperatively managed, the very orbits we now depend upon for navigation, weather forecasting and global communication could one day, perhaps within the lifetimes of people now living, become simply too dangerous to use at all.
statements: One high-speed impact can set off a self-feeding chain reaction that generates ever more pieces of debris.
Reaching agreement on who ought to fund the removal of orbital junk has proved a thorny diplomatic problem.
Abandoned satellites and the discarded upper stages of rockets are among the chief sources of the debris.
Engineers are trialling devices ranging from nets and harpoons to lasers in order to clear away the junk.
Because of its tremendous velocity, even a minute fragment can knock out a functioning satellite.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(单次高速碰撞引发自我增殖的级联) S2→F(谁出资清理难以达成外交共识) S3→B(废弃卫星与火箭残骸是主要来源) S4→E(用网/鱼叉/激光清除) S5→C(高速使微小碎片也能摧毁卫星)。A/G 未匹配。强同义改写。

sets.passage:
[A] As wild bees decline alarmingly across the open countryside, victims of intensive farming and the vanishing of hedgerows, scientists have made a genuinely unexpected and rather cheering discovery: the modern city, of all places, may now be one of the safest and most welcoming habitats left for these vital insects to live in and to thrive.

[B] The central reason is sheer variety. A dense patchwork of private gardens, public parks, window boxes and planted balconies together offers urban bees a far wider range of different flowering plants, blooming across far more of the year, than does a vast agricultural field that has been planted, edge to edge, with nothing whatever but a single uniform crop.

[C] Cities are certainly not without their dangers, of course. The pesticides that residents spray on their flowerbeds, and the steady, relentless loss of small green corners to new building and development, both continue to threaten urban pollinators, so the real picture is not one of simple, untroubled safety but rather of a fragile and easily upset balance.

[D] Ordinary residents, encouragingly, can help a very great deal. Planting native wildflowers, the kind that local bees have evolved alongside over millennia, and simply leaving one wild, untidy, unmown corner of a garden to grow as it pleases, can together transform even a small and ordinary back garden into a genuine and surprisingly valuable refuge for struggling pollinators. Even a single window box, thoughtfully planted with the right mixture of flowers, can provide food at a moment in the year when little else is in bloom, and a row of such boxes running along a street can together form an invisible chain of feeding stations stretching right across a whole neighbourhood, quietly linking one small garden to the next.

[E] One fashionable trend, however, may quietly backfire. The recent craze for keeping decorative rooftop beehives can flood a small area with large numbers of managed honeybees, which then compete directly for a limited supply of flowers with the very wild bees, far less celebrated and far more vulnerable, that are actually most in need of protection.

[F] City planners, meanwhile, are beginning to respond in thoughtful and deliberate ways. They are increasingly weaving pollinator-friendly planting into the careful design of new parks, roadside verges and housing developments, recognising that small, well-judged choices made early, at the drawing board, can make a real and lasting difference to the wildlife of a city.

[G] The broader lesson to be drawn from all of this is an unexpectedly hopeful one: that with only a little forethought and goodwill, the crowded human city and the fragile insect world need not be enemies at all, but may in fact become surprisingly good and mutually beneficial neighbours.
statements: The vogue for rooftop beehives can flood an area with honeybees that compete with the wild bees needing protection.
A patchwork of gardens and parks supplies a greater variety of flowers than a field given over to a single crop.
Planners are now designing pollinator-friendly planting into new parks and housing developments.
Garden pesticides and the steady disappearance of green corners continue to endanger city pollinators.
Householders can assist by growing native flowers and leaving an untidy corner to grow wild.
sets.explain_zh: S1→E(屋顶养蜂热以蜜蜂挤压野生蜂) S2→B(花园公园比单一作物花更多样) S3→F(规划纳入传粉者友好种植) S4→C(农药与绿地丧失威胁城市传粉者) S5→D(种本地花、留野生角落)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] False information has always existed, in every age and every society, but the extraordinary speed and global reach of the internet have together turned it into one of the genuinely defining problems of our own time. What a rumour-monger once whispered to a handful of neighbours can now reach millions of strangers across the world in a matter of minutes, with consequences no one can fully control.

[B] Careful studies repeatedly find that falsehoods often travel faster and further than the plain truth, and the reason is revealing. False stories are frequently crafted, deliberately or instinctively, to provoke a strong and immediate emotion, whether outrage, fear or delight, and it is precisely such emotionally charged material that we are most quick to pass along to others without pausing to check it. A sober correction or a careful, balanced account, by contrast, rarely stirs the same fierce feeling, and so it tends to spread far more slowly and to reach far fewer people, however true and important it may happen to be. The result is a strange and troubling imbalance, in which the most exciting version of events, rather than the most accurate one, reliably wins the largest audience and lodges most firmly in the public mind.

[C] The platforms themselves make matters considerably worse. Their recommendation algorithms, designed above all to maximise the time users spend engaged, tend automatically to amplify and spread whatever content is most sensational, surprising or provocative, rather than whatever happens to be most accurate, balanced or genuinely useful to the people reading it.

[D] Our own minds, uncomfortably, are part of the problem too. Through a deep mental habit that psychologists call 'confirmation bias', we readily and almost unconsciously believe claims that happen to fit what we already think is true, while we instinctively doubt, dismiss or explain away any evidence that inconveniently contradicts our existing beliefs.

[E] Education offers one of the more promising defences. Patiently teaching people, especially the young, to check the sources of what they read, to weigh competing evidence and to recognise manipulation, a set of skills sometimes called media literacy, can over time make ordinary readers significantly harder to fool and to mislead.

[F] Simply deleting offending content, by contrast, is no real cure on its own. Unless people genuinely learn to think critically for themselves, banned falsehoods merely vanish for a moment and then reappear, lightly disguised, in new forms and on new platforms, as fast as the old ones can possibly be removed.

[G] The healthiest possible information climate, in the end, depends far less on what is forcibly removed from public view than on what ordinary readers are themselves equipped, and willing, to question. The most durable defence against falsehood, in short, has finally to be built inside the reader's own questioning mind.
statements: We readily accept claims that match our existing beliefs and instinctively reject those that contradict them.
Falsehoods spread quickly largely because they are crafted to provoke a strong emotional reaction.
Merely deleting content fails unless people genuinely learn to think critically for themselves.
Platform algorithms amplify the most sensational content rather than the most accurate.
Media literacy teaches people to check their sources and weigh competing evidence.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(确认偏误:信符合己见、拒相反证据) S2→B(假信息因情绪化而传播快) S3→F(仅删除无用,须批判思维) S4→C(算法放大耸动而非准确内容) S5→E(媒介素养教人核查来源)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Once a marginal and slightly suspect convenience, the remote medical consultation has, in the space of just a few short years, become a familiar and accepted part of how many of us now receive everyday care. A meeting that once had to happen face to face, in a clinic, can increasingly take place through a screen, and both patients and doctors are steadily growing accustomed to the change.

[B] Its single greatest promise is reach. A simple video call can effectively bring a distant specialist directly to a patient in a remote rural village, someone who might otherwise have to travel for many hours over poor roads to reach the nearest hospital, or who, daunted by the journey and the cost, might in practice never make the trip and seek help at all.

[C] It saves a great deal of time as well, for everyone involved. For routine and minor matters, a short and focused telephone or video call can neatly replace an entire half-day otherwise lost to crowded waiting rooms, long queues and tiring travel, a welcome and meaningful saving for the busy patient and the overstretched doctor alike. Over the course of a whole year, the hours saved in this quiet way add up to something genuinely significant, freeing scarce clinical time for the patients who truly do need to be examined in person, and sparing those with only minor complaints the considerable trouble, expense and lost income of an unnecessary journey right across town merely to sit waiting in a crowded room.

[D] But the method has clear and important limits. A doctor simply cannot physically examine a patient through a screen, cannot feel, listen or test in the ordinary way, and there remain a great many conditions which, by their very nature, cannot be properly or safely assessed from a distance, however good the camera and the connection may be.

[E] Access, frustratingly, is also uneven. The elderly and the poor, who often need medical care the most urgently, are frequently the very people who lack the necessary devices, the fast and reliable internet connection, or simply the quiet confidence that using remote consultation comfortably and effectively tends to demand of a patient.

[F] Privacy is a further and growing worry. Sensitive personal medical data, once it is transmitted across the internet and stored on remote servers, inevitably becomes one more tempting target for theft, leakage or commercial misuse, in a way that a paper file locked in a clinic drawer never quite was.

[G] Remote medicine, then, is best understood and used not as a wholesale replacement for the traditional clinic, but rather as a genuinely valuable and humane extension of it, widening its reach without pretending to do everything.
statements: A doctor simply cannot carry out a physical examination of a patient through a screen.
A video call can effectively bring a distant specialist to a patient in a remote village.
The elderly and the poor may lack the devices, connection or confidence the method demands.
Personal medical data sent across the internet and stored online becomes a target for misuse.
For routine matters, a short call can replace a half-day lost to travel and crowded waiting rooms.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(屏幕无法体格检查) S2→B(把专家带给偏远村庄患者) S3→E(老人穷人缺设备/网络/信心) S4→F(在线医疗数据成盗用目标) S5→C(常规问题省去差旅候诊)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] To read a script that no living person now speaks, written in a language that no one has heard aloud for thousands of years, is among the most quietly romantic of all scholarly tasks. The decipherer attempts, in effect, to make the long-dead speak again, coaxing meaning from marks that have kept their secrets, silent and patient, across the whole span of recorded history.

[B] The classic key to such a puzzle is a bilingual text. The famous Rosetta Stone, which by good fortune repeated the very same royal decree in one already-known language alongside an unknown one, at last allowed scholars to crack the mystery of Egyptian hieroglyphs, matching the familiar words against the strange symbols until the symbols slowly began to speak.

[C] Where no such convenient bilingual text happens to survive, scholars must instead build a bridge of an altogether different kind. They search patiently for a known, related language, perhaps a descendant or a cousin of the lost one, whose surviving words may distantly echo in the undeciphered script and offer the first faint, precious footholds toward a reading.

[D] Computers, in recent decades, have become powerful and tireless allies in this delicate work. Specialised programs can rapidly sift through vast numbers of surviving inscriptions, searching for the subtle, recurring patterns and statistical regularities that no single human eye, however brilliant or patient, could ever hope to hold and compare all at once.

[E] Some scripts, however, stubbornly resist every effort that is made against them. Linear A, the mysterious writing used by the ancient Minoans of Crete, has quietly defeated more than a full century of brilliant, dedicated and ingenious attempts at decipherment, and to this day it remains entirely unread and silent.

[F] Success, when it finally comes, brings a rare and remarkable reward. A newly decoded script can suddenly fill a long-silent civilisation with living voices, abruptly revealing its laws and its kings, its trade and its taxes, its myths, its quarrels and even its most private prayers, where before there had been only an enigmatic silence. A people known previously only through their broken pots and their silent, roofless ruins can, at the turning of a single key, begin once again to speak in their own words, naming their gods, recording their harvests, and complaining, across the long centuries, about their troublesome neighbours. For the patient scholars who devote whole careers to such work, that sudden rush of recovered human voices is a reward quite unlike any other in the whole of learning.

[G] Decipherment, at its very best, is therefore less a cold scholarly triumph over the past than it is a long-interrupted human conversation, patiently and at last, finally resumed.
statements: Specialised computer programs can rapidly sift through huge numbers of inscriptions for recurring patterns.
A bilingual text repeating the same decree in a known language proved the key to hieroglyphs.
Some scripts, such as the Minoans' Linear A, remain unread despite a century of effort.
A decoded script can suddenly reveal a lost civilisation's laws, trade and private prayers.
Without a bilingual text, scholars must rely on a known, related language for footholds.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(计算机筛查铭文找模式) S2→B(双语对照同一法令是破译关键) S3→E(线形文字A 历经百年仍未解) S4→F(破译揭示失落文明的法律/贸易/祈祷) S5→C(无双语文本则依赖已知亲属语言)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Climate change is almost always pictured as something happening in the air, a matter of rising temperatures, melting glaciers and violent storms. Yet some of its most profound and far-reaching effects are taking place, quietly and largely out of sight, in the vast body of the sea, where a slow chemical transformation is under way that few people ever notice.

[B] As the world's oceans steadily absorb a large share of the carbon dioxide that human activity releases into the atmosphere, that gas dissolves into the seawater and, through a straightforward chemical reaction, slowly but measurably turns the water more acidic, a global change that scientists can now track reliably from one end of the planet to the other.

[C] For a great many small marine creatures, this gradual acidification is genuinely dangerous. Shellfish, corals and countless tiny planktonic organisms build their protective shells and delicate skeletons out of certain dissolved minerals, and water that has grown more acidic makes those very minerals significantly harder to extract and to form into solid structures. Creatures that have patiently built their armour in the very same way for many millions of years suddenly find the basic chemistry of their own bodies working quietly against them, their shells growing thinner, weaker and slower to form. For the reef-building corals in particular, which construct the vast living structures that shelter perhaps a quarter of all marine species, this slow chemical shift threatens not merely scattered individual animals but whole ecosystems on which countless other creatures silently depend for food and shelter.

[D] The effect, troublingly, ripples steadily upward through the whole system. As the smallest organisms at the very base of the marine food web begin to struggle and decline, the larger fish, and ultimately the great commercial fisheries and the human communities that all depend upon them, are placed quietly but seriously at risk of collapse.

[E] Acidification, moreover, rarely acts alone or in isolation. Combined with the simultaneous and relentless warming of the seas, it compounds and multiplies the stress placed on marine ecosystems that have, in many regions, already been pushed close to the very limits of what they can endure and still recover from.

[F] There is, frustratingly, no easy local fix for the problem. Because its ultimate cause is the sheer global volume of carbon emissions, only a genuine worldwide reduction of those emissions can ever address ocean acidification at its true root; no amount of local effort can neutralise an entire ocean.

[G] The quietly acidifying ocean stands, in the end, as a powerful reminder that the atmosphere above and the sea below are not two separate worlds at all, but in truth a single, deeply connected and interdependent system.
statements: Only a genuine worldwide cut in carbon emissions can address the problem at its true root.
Shellfish and corals find it markedly harder to build their shells in more acidic water.
Dissolved carbon dioxide slowly and measurably makes seawater more acidic across the globe.
Combined with warming seas, acidification multiplies the stress on already strained ecosystems.
As the smallest organisms decline, the larger fish and fisheries above them are put at risk.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(全球减排才是治本) S2→C(酸性水中贝类珊瑚更难造壳) S3→B(溶入CO2使全球海水变酸) S4→E(与变暖叠加加压) S5→D(底层生物受困殃及上层鱼类渔业)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] The once-radical idea of paying every single citizen a regular, unconditional sum of money, with no strings attached, an arrangement known as a 'universal basic income', has in recent years moved steadily from the distant fringes of political debate toward its very centre, taken seriously now by economists and politicians who would once have dismissed it out of hand.

[B] Its supporters advance several arguments. They contend that such a payment would sweep away and simplify the present bewildering tangle of separate welfare programmes, with all their bureaucracy and stigma, and would at the same time give every person a basic, unconditional security, a solid floor beneath them, from which they could afford to take risks, retrain, or search calmly for better work. Freed from the constant, grinding fear of outright destitution, they argue, ordinary people would be far better able to refuse genuinely exploitative jobs, to start small businesses of their own, to care unpaid for ageing relatives, or to study patiently for years toward some distant goal, and society as a whole would ultimately gain from the creativity and the steady security that such a guaranteed floor beneath everyone might, in time, quietly release.

[C] A newer and increasingly prominent argument points to automation. As intelligent machines and software take over more and more of the jobs that people currently do, some thinkers argue, a guaranteed basic income may eventually become necessary simply to support the large numbers of people whose traditional work has been quietly automated out of existence altogether.

[D] Opponents, however, tend to reply with a single decisive word: cost. Paying every citizen, rich and poor alike, a sum genuinely large enough to live on would, they argue, demand truly colossal amounts of public money, sums so vast that no realistic government could ever hope to raise and sustain them year after year without ruinous taxation.

[E] A common further fear is that, freed from the need to earn, large numbers of people would simply stop working altogether, although the small-scale practical trials conducted so far have, somewhat reassuringly, generally found the actual effect on overall employment to be surprisingly modest and limited.

[F] Those trials, however, are genuinely hard to interpret with confidence. Run only for a few short years, and among only a few thousand carefully selected people who know the scheme is temporary, they cannot easily or reliably predict what a permanent, nationwide and truly universal programme would actually do to a whole society's behaviour.

[G] Whatever one finally concludes about its merits, the very vigour of the debate itself reflects a deeper and more enduring question: namely, what exactly a society owes to each of its members in an age of bewilderingly rapid economic change.
statements: Some argue a guaranteed basic income may become necessary as automation removes traditional jobs.
Opponents focus above all on the colossal cost of paying everyone enough to live on.
Supporters say it would simplify the tangle of welfare and provide a basic security.
Small-scale trials have generally found only a modest effect on overall employment.
Limited, temporary trials cannot reliably predict the effect of a permanent nationwide scheme.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(自动化夺岗或需UBI) S2→D(反对者聚焦巨大成本) S3→B(简化福利+提供基本保障) S4→E(小试验对就业影响有限) S5→F(有限临时试验难预测全国永久方案)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] In the world's great national museums, a long-simmering argument has lately grown loud and impossible to ignore: should the cultural treasures that were taken away, often long ago and far away, now be returned to the lands and the peoples from which they originally came? The question, once politely avoided, now demands an answer.

[B] Many of the most contested such objects arrived in these museums during the colonial era, carried off either by outright force during conquest, or else acquired under deeply unequal and coercive terms of trade that very few people today would seriously attempt to defend as fair or just by any modern standard.

[C] In recent years, the call for the return of these objects has grown steadily louder and more insistent, voiced now not only by the governments of the countries of origin, but also by respected scholars, by activists, and by the living descendants of the very people who first made and used the treasures in question. For these communities, the objects are very far from being mere curiosities behind glass in some distant foreign capital; they are often sacred items, ancestral human remains, or the central symbols of a whole national identity, torn away at a moment of weakness and never once freely given. To see them displayed abroad, neatly labelled and admired by passing strangers, can feel less like a generous celebration of shared world culture than a fresh daily reminder of an old and still unhealed injury.

[D] The holding museums, for their part, often resist these demands strenuously. They argue that they preserve fragile and irreplaceable objects with great expertise and care, that they display them freely to a vast global public who could otherwise never see them, and that, in a good many cases, they hold the objects entirely legally.

[E] A middle path is sometimes proposed as a compromise. High-quality digital reproductions, or alternatively generous long-term loans between institutions, might in principle allow a single contested object to be both genuinely returned to its homeland and yet, in some form, still remain widely seen and studied elsewhere in the world.

[F] The general mood, nonetheless, is unmistakably shifting. A growing number of prominent museums have now actually begun to return important and symbolic works to their countries of origin, quietly accepting in practice that rightful ownership is not, and never was, a purely legal question to be settled by old paperwork alone.

[G] At its very heart, this difficult debate asks a profound and unsettling question: to whom does a genuinely shared human heritage truly belong, and who, in the end, has the moral right to decide its fate? There is no simple or comfortable answer, but the question itself can no longer be quietly set aside and ignored.
statements: Many contested objects were taken during the colonial era by force or under unequal terms.
Museums argue that they preserve fragile objects expertly and display them to a global public.
The call for the return of such objects has grown louder in recent years.
High-quality digital copies or long-term loans are proposed as a compromise middle path.
A growing number of museums have now actually begun to return important works.
sets.explain_zh: S1→B(殖民时期以武力/不平等取得) S2→D(博物馆称专业保存+面向全球展出) S3→C(归还呼声近年增强) S4→E(数字复制/长期借展作折中) S5→F(部分博物馆已开始归还)。A/G 未匹配。
